observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Accounting Office:
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct all affected accounts; and
observe the proper recording of transactions to prevent posting erroneous information in the financial reports rendered by the municipality.

We recommended that management continuously require and monitor the current practice of consistently depositing collections on time to ensure adherence to the rules and regulations outlined in Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, pertaining to collections and deposits.

We recommended that the Accountable Officer ensure the presentation of all undisbursed cash advances during the cash count for consideration by the audit to ensure accurate computation of accountability. Henceforth, strict adherence to P.D. No. 1445 is enjoined.

We recommended that management reassess the security infrastructure of the Office of the Treasurer. This should encompass various considerations, including, but not limited to, the following:
Enhance Door and Window Security: Install reinforced doors and windows with appropriate security features to enhance resistance against break-ins.
Secure Partition Room and Cabinet: Replace wooden partitions with more secure materials and upgrade the cabinet to steel for better protection of accountable forms and money.
Upgrade Cash Vault Capacity: Consider expanding the capacity of the cash vault to accommodate larger amounts of money, providing a secure storage solution for various instances.

We recommended that:
the Municipal Accountant  prepare Bank Reconciliation Statements for all months where they are missing or incomplete;
the Municipal Accountant to prepare journal entry vouchers to adjust all valid reconciling items identified in the BRS, including stale checks; and
the Municipal Treasury Office strictly follow the process outlined in the MNGAS to manage stale checks. This includes timely notification to payees and proper adjustment in the financial records to reflect liabilities and assets accurately.

We recommended that management require the:
Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) and Investment Property. This includes a reconciliation of the physical count with the property and accounting records, as required by Chapter 4 of the Handbook on Property & Supply Management System and Paragraph 4 of Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. Any discrepancies should be thoroughly investigated, cleared, and reconciled immediately and written explanations be obtained from accountable and responsible persons if necessary.
The procedures for one-time cleansing of PPE account balances is recommended per COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
Accounting Office and General Services Office to enhance documentation and record-keeping practices related to PPE and Investment Property by preparing and continuously maintaining and updating PPE ledger cards and property cards, respectively. Both offices  ensure that all movements of PPE are properly recorded and documented to facilitate accurate reconciliation and reporting; and
Concerned members of the Inventory Committee to undergo training on proper inventory procedures, reconciliation processes, and record-keeping practices, if necessary.

We recommended that:
the Management create a committee/team to:
a.1 facilitate the titling of 24 parcels of land in the name of the Municipality and thereafter provide the Accounting Office with copies thereof for recording in the books of accounts; and
a.2 determine the mode of acquisition of lots of the Municipality. In case of donation, the committee/team should determine the fair market value thereof which will be recorded in the books of accounts; and
the Municipal Accountant record the parcels of land owned by the Municipality following the provisions of IPSAS 17 and to provide detailed schedule for verification, monitoring and reconciliation purposes.

We recommended that:
The LGU continues to monitor the progress of the projects to ensure that they remain on track and are completed within the stipulated time frame;
The implementing offices provide regular updates and reports on the status of the projects, including any challenges or deviations from the plan and the actions taken to address them; and
The LGU ensures continued adherence to the terms outlined in the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or other relevant documents governing the utilization of the funds. Should the municipality wish to repurpose the property or facilities, it must obtain authorization from the grantor/source agency.

We recommended that Management:
prioritize and accelerate the implementation of its ongoing projects funded by the 20% MDF to ensure that the allocated funds are utilized efficiently and effectively and ensure timely delivery of benefits to the community, such as improved infrastructure, services, and economic opportunities;
continuously align development projects funded by the 20% Development Fund (DF) with the Municipality's development goals and the needs of its constituents. Projects should be selected based on their potential to contribute to efficient and effective local governance, as well as their ability to promote the general welfare of the people; and
ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready to avoid delays, cost overruns, and other issues that may arise during project execution.

We recommended that management adhere strictly to the appropriate procurement procedures for all transactions and avoid resorting to emergency purchases unless the specified conditions are met in accordance with procurement regulations.

We recommended that management:
enforce strict adherence to the guidelines provided in the APP and the PPMPs to ensure all procurements are consolidated where feasible. This will optimize the use of resources and reduce unnecessary administrative expenditures;
require concerned officials and employees to undergo training on the importance of following the procurement planning and consolidation processes as outlined in the Government Procurement Reform Act and its IRR; and
implement a monitoring mechanism to assess compliance with the APP regularly and to ensure that deviations are addressed promptly. This should include a periodic assessment of procurement activities to ensure alignment with the planned and consolidated procurement activities.

We recommended that management:
conduct a thorough review of current procurement procedures to identify and rectify gaps or weaknesses that may allow for non-compliance;
instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure that the Request for Quotations are sent only to suppliers or contractors of known qualifications; and
ensure all procurement activities strictly adhere to R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR.

We recommended that management implement the following measures:
Notice to Proceed:
Ensure that all Notice to Proceed documents include the date of receipt by the contractor. In cases where the date is missing, consider the date of the Notice to Proceed itself as the date of receipt.
Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA):
Implement a policy requiring the preparation of two SWAs for each project: one as of the supposed end of the contract duration and another as of the actual date of project completion. This will facilitate the accurate computation of liquidated damages against the contractor.
Preparation of SWA:
Establish a process where the SWA is prepared by the contractor and subsequently validated and/or confirmed by the procuring entity. This will ensure the accuracy and reliability of the SWA.
Certificate of Completion and Certificate of Acceptance:
Enforce the separation of the Certificate of Acceptance from the Certificate of Completion in project documentation. Additionally, ensure that the Certificate of Acceptance is issued no earlier than one year from the project's completion date.
Suspension of Work and Extension of Contract Time:
Strengthen oversight to ensure that necessary documentary requirements support all instances of work suspensions/extensions. Non-compliance should result in the invalidation of the contract extension and the imposition of applicable liquidated damages in case of unjustified delays.
Non-imposition of Liquidated Damages:
Review all project contracts to ensure that liquidated damages are properly imposed for delays in project completion. Enforce the provisions of the contract agreements and the procurement law to hold contractors accountable for delays.
Submission of Procurement Documents:
Implement a system to track the submission of all procurement documents and documentary requirements on advance payment, progress payment, and final payment of infrastructure projects. Ensure that all required documents are submitted promptly to avoid delays in audit and inspection of projects.
Require personnel involved in procurement and project monitoring to undergo training and capacity-building activities to enhance their understanding of requirements and procedures.

We recommended that:
the local chief executive require the BAC/BAC Secretariat to submit the missing documents immediately. Otherwise, we will be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension pursuant to COA rules and regulations;
to ensure that all lacking documents are submitted, the BAC Secretariat should accomplish the attached checklist of documentary requirements and put index tabs/tabbings on the documents to be submitted; and
the Bids and Awards Committee, to ensure that all supporting contract documents are complete in compliance with pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR, and submitted within five (5) days from execution of contracts in accordance with Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that:
within a period not exceeding fifteen (15) calendar days from the determination by the BAC of the bidder with the LCRB, HRRB, SCRB, or SRRB, and the recommendation to award the contract, the HoPE or his duly authorized representative shall approve or disapprove the said recommendation and immediately issue the Notice of Award to ensure the early implementation of projects; and
management implement a monitoring system to track the progress of procurement activities and ensure that all steps, including the issuance of Notices of Award, are completed within the prescribed timelines. The Procurement Monitoring Report (PMR) should be continuously prepared, maintained and updated to serve as a guide.

We recommended that:
the BAC/BAC Secretariat strictly observe Section 37.4 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 and ensure that the Notice to Proceed is issued and received by the successful bidders within seven (7) calendar days from the date of contract approval; and
management implement a monitoring system to track the progress of procurement activities and ensure that all steps, including the issuance of Notices to Proceed, are completed within the prescribed timelines. The Procurement Monitoring Report (PMR) should be continuously prepared, maintained and updated to serve as a guide.

We recommended that the TWG and BAC strictly conduct post-qualification of bids to ensure that contracts should be awarded only to bidders who are responsive to the project's requirements and evaluate the bids offered per pertinent provisions of Section 32 of 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended that:
Management prepare and implement guidelines governing the grant of communication allowances to officials and employees. These guidelines should clearly outline:
Eligibility criteria for receiving communication allowance.
Allocation limits per user based on the nature of their duties and responsibilities.
Monthly ceilings/caps on communication expenses to ensure expenditures remain within budgetary constraints.
Modes of availment, including options such as postpaid plans, prepaid allocations, or cell card usage.
Procedures for obtaining approval and documentation requirements for reimbursement.
The prevailing rates and load promos offered by mobile networks should be considered.
The Municipal Accountant require personnel in the school districts to submit a certification confirming that they had not received a similar allowance from their respective primary agencies, the same to be attached to the payroll; and
The Municipal Accountant shall ensure the completeness of supporting documents. In the case of prepaid card users, the submission of used cards should be required prior to the processing and disbursement of communication allowance, ensuring the appropriate utilization of government resources.

The Municipal Accountant has analyzed discrepancies related to the timing differences between the recording of fund transfers from the General Fund to the Trust Fund. These discrepancies, amounting to P3,735,136, occurred because the transfer was recorded in the General Fund's books but not in the Trust Fund's books at year-end. Adjustments are being made to correct the "Due from Other Funds" figures under the Trust Fund Account. The office is also addressing discrepancies from prior years and is in the process of identifying the nature of each transaction to make the necessary corrections to the entries.

The Municipal Accountant's office maintains a subsidiary ledger for transactions under the "Due to Other Funds" for both the General Fund and the Trust Fund. The office commits to maintaining these ledgers and adhering to the relevant provisions of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 1, specifically Paragraph 27, and Section 112 of Presidential Decree (P.D.) No. 1445, regarding the disclosure of these accounts.

We again recommended that management:
require the Municipal Accountant to verify and validate the existence of these receivables, determine the debtors, and assess their collectability. If deemed uncollectible, request authority to write-off the dormant receivable accounts following COA Circular No. 2016-005.; and
ensure that subsidiary ledgers are maintained for receivables in accordance with Section 4(e) of the NGAS to provide detailed information on accounts and facilitate collection efforts in the future.

We again recommended that:
management should immediately demand the settlement of all long-overdue cash advances.
management should strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances;
the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to cause the withholding of the salary of Accountable Officers whose previous cash advances were not liquidated on or before January 20 of the ensuing year pursuant to Item 5.9 of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the Rules and Regulations on the Granting, Utilization and Liquidation of Cash Advances; and
the Municipal Accountant should request the write-off of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 and COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023, which prescribed the Guidelines and Procedures on the Proper Disposition/Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, and Fund Transfers.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough review of the transactions causing the negative balances and make appropriate adjustments to ensure the accuracy and valuation of the accounts.

We recommended that management:
ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160;
require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund; and
ensure that the cash accruing from the Due from Other Funds is collected in full.

We reiterate our recommendation that management should reassess its practice of providing financial assistance to other LGUs, particularly those of higher income classification. Management should consider engaging in discussions with other LGUs in the Province to evaluate the practice and, if deemed necessary, discontinue it.

We recommended that the Accounting Office:

prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct all affected accounts; and

observe due diligence in recording its transactions to prevent posting erroneous information in the financial reports rendered by the Municipality.
